questions.question:
Write address intermediate code (quadruples) for the following boolean expression in the sequence as it would be generated by a compiler. Partial evaluation of boolean expressions is not permitted. Assume the usual rules of precedence of the operators.

(a + b) > (c + d) or a > c and b < d
questions.to_find: Three-address intermediate code (quadruples) for the boolean expression (a+b) > (c+d) or a > c and b < d

understand.plain: We need to generate three-address intermediate code (quadruples) for a boolean expression. Since partial evaluation (short-circuit) is NOT permitted, every sub-expression must be fully evaluated. The 'and' operator has higher precedence than 'or', so 'a > c and b < d' is evaluated first as a group, then or-ed with '(a+b) > (c+d)'.

keywords.explain: A three-address code representation with four fields: (operator, operand1, operand2, result). Each quadruple performs exactly one operation.
keywords.example: (+, a, b, t1) means t1 = a + b

keywords.explain: Both operands of 'and' and 'or' must be fully computed before combining. No short-circuit: if left side of 'or' is true, right side is still evaluated.
keywords.example: For 'X or Y', compute X, then compute Y, then compute X or Y — even if X is already true.

keywords.explain: In the expression, arithmetic > relational > 'and' > 'or'. So 'and' binds tighter than 'or', and arithmetic (+) is computed before relational (>).
keywords.example: a > c and b < d is parsed as (a > c) and (b < d)

stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ink' font-size='14'>> (t3)</text><text x='420' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ink' font-size='14'>and (t6)</text><line x1='70' y1='115' x2='50' y2='145' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='115' x2='150' y2='145' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='390' y1='115' x2='370' y2='145' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='450' y1='115' x2='470' y2='145' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='50' y='160' text-anchor='middle' class='ink' font-size='13'>+(t1)</text><text x='150' y='160' font-size='13'><(t5)</text><text x='30' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='mut' font-size='12'>a,b</text><text x='170' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='mut' font-size='12'>c,d</text><text x='350' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='mut' font-size='12'>a,c</text><text x='490' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='mut' font-size='12'>b,d</text><text x='260' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Post-order traversal gives the quadruple sequence</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree showing the boolean expression: 'or' at root, left child is '> (t3)' with children '+ (t1): a,b' and '+ (t2): c,d', right child is 'and (t6)' with children '> (t4): a,c' and '< (t5): b,d'. Post-order traversal of this tree gives the order of quadruples.

given.aim: Generate all quadruples for (a+b) > (c+d) or a > c and b < d without short-circuit evaluation

terms.meaning: (a+b) > (c+d) or a > c and b < d
terms.example: With a=3,b=1,c=2,d=2: (4>4)=false, (3>2 and 1<2)=true, false or true = true
terms.connects: This is the expression to be compiled into quadruples

terms.meaning: All sub-expressions are evaluated regardless of intermediate results
terms.example: Even if (a+b)>(c+d) is true, we still compute 'a>c and b<d'
terms.connects: Forces us to emit code for all 7 operations

terms.meaning: 'and' binds tighter than 'or' in boolean algebra, just like * binds tighter than +
terms.example: X or Y and Z = X or (Y and Z)
terms.connects: Determines that t6 = and(t4,t5) is computed before t7 = or(t3,t6)

terms.meaning: Each intermediate result is stored in a compiler-generated temporary
terms.example: t1 = a+b stores the first arithmetic result
terms.connects: Allows each quadruple to reference previous results

given.plan: Traverse the expression parse tree in post-order (compute children before parent). First compute arithmetic sub-expressions (a+b, c+d), then relational comparisons, then 'and' (higher precedence than 'or'), finally 'or'.

steps.title: Parse and order sub-expressions
steps.apply:
Expression tree (bottom-up):
- t1 = a + b (left arithmetic)
- t2 = c + d (right arithmetic)
- t3 = t1 > t2 (left relational)
- t4 = a > c (for 'and' branch)
- t5 = b < d (for 'and' branch)
- t6 = t4 and t5
- t7 = t3 or t6
steps.note: The 'and' sub-expression (a>c and b<d) is evaluated completely before 'or' combines both sides.

steps.title: Emit quadruples in order
steps.note: Seven quadruples total: 2 additions, 3 relational ops, 1 and, 1 or. Result t7 holds the final boolean value.

questions.formula_note: Three-address code / quadruple generation via post-order traversal of the expression parse tree; operator precedence determines parse order.

questions.question:
Generation of intermediate code based on an abstract machine model is useful in compilers because

A. it makes implementation of lexical analysis and syntax analysis easier
B. syntax-directed translations can be written for intermediate code generation
C. it enhances the portability of the front end of the compiler
D. it is not possible to directly generate machine code from the source language
questions.answer: C. it enhances the portability of the front end of the compiler
questions.to_find: Why is intermediate code generation based on an abstract machine model useful in compilers?

understand.plain: Intermediate code (like three-address code or P-code) sits between the source language and the target machine code. The question asks why generating it based on an abstract machine model is specifically beneficial. The key insight is that by targeting an abstract (not real) machine, the compiler's back end can be easily replaced to target different real machines — improving portability.

keywords.explain: An abstract representation of a program, between source code and final machine code (e.g., three-address code, bytecode, P-code).
keywords.example: Java bytecode is intermediate code run by the JVM abstract machine.

keywords.explain: The ability to retarget the compiler to a new hardware platform by only replacing the back end (code generator) while keeping the front end (parser, semantic analyzer, IR generator) unchanged.
keywords.example: A compiler for C can target x86, ARM, or MIPS by changing only the back end if it uses IR.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><rect x='10' y='50' width='110' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='65' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Source</text><text x='65' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>(.c / .java)</text><polygon points='130,75 150,65 150,85' class='ok'/><rect x='155' y='50' width='100' height='50' rx='6' fill='#E8F5E9' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='205' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Front End</text><text x='205' y='90' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>IR / IC</text><polygon points='265,75 285,65 285,85' class='ok'/><rect x='290' y='10' width='90' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='290' y='60' width='90' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><rect x='290' y='110' width='90' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='335' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>x86</text><text x='335' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>ARM</text><text x='335' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>MIPS</text><line x1='285' y1='75' x2='290' y2='30' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='285' y1='75' x2='290' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='285' y1='75' x2='290' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='380' y='80' class='sub mut' font-size='13'>Back Ends</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Compiler pipeline: Source code goes through the Front End to produce Intermediate Code (IR). The IR is then fed into multiple Back Ends targeting different machines (x86, ARM, MIPS). The front end is reused across all targets, illustrating portability.

given.aim: Identify the primary benefit of using an abstract machine model for intermediate code generation

terms.meaning: A hypothetical, idealized processor that intermediate code targets — not tied to any real hardware
terms.example: The JVM is an abstract machine; Java compiles to JVM bytecode
terms.connects: Intermediate code targeting an abstract machine is machine-independent

terms.meaning: Lexer + parser + semantic analyzer + IR generator — processes the source language
terms.example: For a C compiler: tokenizer, C grammar parser, type checker, three-address code emitter
terms.connects: The front end produces IR and does not change when targeting a new machine

terms.meaning: Code generator + optimizer that translates IR to actual machine instructions
terms.example: Converting three-address code to x86 assembly
terms.connects: Only the back end needs to change when porting to a new hardware platform

given.plan: Eliminate wrong options: (A) IR doesn't help lexical/syntax analysis. (B) Syntax-directed translation helps in parsing, not specifically because of abstract machine model. (D) It IS possible to directly generate machine code (one-pass compilers do this). (C) is correct — abstract machine IR separates front from back end, allowing the front end to be reused across multiple target architectures.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply:
A. Lexical and syntax analysis are done before IR generation; IR doesn't make them easier — WRONG.
B. Syntax-directed translations for IC generation are a technique, not a benefit of the abstract machine model per se — WRONG.
D. Direct machine code generation IS possible (e.g., one-pass compilers) — WRONG.
steps.note: The question specifically asks about the abstract machine model for intermediate code, not about IR in general.

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: By targeting an abstract machine, the front end (lexer, parser, semantic analysis, IR generation) becomes machine-independent. When porting to a new target hardware, only the back end needs to be rewritten. This significantly enhances the portability of the front end — it can be shared across all target platforms.
steps.note: This is the classic argument for using IR in compilers: write once (front end), retarget many times (back ends).

solution.result: C. it enhances the portability of the front end of the compiler

questions.formula_note: Intermediate representation (IR) as a machine-independent pivot allows front-end reuse and back-end portability across target architectures.

questions.question:
For a C program accessing X[i][j][k], the following intermediate code is generated by a compiler. Assume that the size of an integer is 32 bits and the size of a character is 8 bits.

t0 = i * 1024
t1 = j * 32
t2 = k * 4
t3 = t1 + t0
t4 = t3 + t2
t5 = X[t4]

Which one of the following statements about the source code for the C program is CORRECT?

A. X is declared as "int X[32][32][8]"
B. X is declared as "int X[1024][32][4]"
C. X is declared as "char X[1024][32][8]"
D. X is declared as "int X[32][16][2]"
questions.answer: A. X is declared as "int X[32][32][8]"
questions.to_find: The correct declaration of 3D array X given the intermediate code for X[i][j][k]

understand.plain: A 3D array X[d1][d2][d3] of type T is stored in row-major order. Accessing X[i][j][k] requires computing the byte offset: i*(d2*d3*sizeof(T)) + j*(d3*sizeof(T)) + k*sizeof(T). The intermediate code gives us the multipliers used for i, j, and k. We need to reverse-engineer the dimensions d1, d2, d3 and element type from these multipliers.

keywords.explain: 3D array elements are stored row by row: X[i][j][k] offset = i*(d2*d3*size) + j*(d3*size) + k*size, where size is the element size in bytes.
keywords.example: For int X[4][4][4] (size=4 bytes): X[1][0][0] is at offset 1*4*4*4 = 64 bytes

keywords.explain: The size of each element in the array: int = 4 bytes (32 bits), char = 1 byte (8 bits).
keywords.example: k*4 in the code means element size = 4 bytes = int

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Row-Major Offset for X[i][j][k]</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='145' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='82' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>i * (d2*d3*size)</text><text x='82' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>= i * 1024</text><rect x='175' y='45' width='145' height='50' rx='6' fill='#E8F5E9' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='247' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>j * (d3*size)</text><text x='247' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>= j * 32</text><rect x='340' y='45' width='145' height='50' rx='6' fill='#FFF3E0' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='412' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>k * size</text><text x='412' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>= k * 4</text><text x='250' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>size = 4 bytes (int) | d3*4 = 32 so d3 = 8 | d2*d3*4 = 1024 so d2 = 32</text><rect x='10' y='145' width='470' height='40' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>int X[d1][32][8] — option A: int X[32][32][8]</text><text x='250' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>d1 can be any value (only i's stride matters, not d1's bound)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing three components of the array offset formula: i*(d2*d3*size)=i*1024, j*(d3*size)=j*32, k*size=k*4. From k*4: element size=4 bytes (int). From j*32: d3*4=32 so d3=8. From i*1024: d2*d3*4=1024, so d2=32. Declaration: int X[?][32][8], matching option A: int X[32][32][8].

given.aim: Determine the declaration of X[i][j][k] from the multipliers used in the generated intermediate code

terms.meaning: The multiplier for the innermost index k is 4 bytes = sizeof(int)
terms.example: char would give k*1; int gives k*4; this confirms element type is int

terms.meaning: Stride for j-dimension = d3 * sizeof(T) = 32 bytes
Stride for i-dimension = d2 * d3 * sizeof(T) = 1024 bytes

given.plan: From k's multiplier extract element size and type. From j's multiplier extract d3. From i's multiplier extract d2. The first dimension d1 doesn't appear in offset calculation directly but is given in the declaration (must be consistent). Match against options.

steps.title: Find element type from k's multiplier
steps.apply:
t2 = k * 4, so sizeof(T) = 4 bytes = 32 bits = int.
This rules out option C (which uses char, sizeof = 1 byte).
steps.note: The innermost dimension's stride equals the element size.

steps.title: Find d3 from j's multiplier
steps.apply:
t1 = j * 32
d3 * 4 = 32
d3 = 32 / 4 = 8
So the third dimension is 8.
steps.note: d3 = 8 rules out option B (which has d3=4) and option D (which has d3=2).

steps.title: Find d2 from i's multiplier
steps.note: With d2=32, d3=8, type=int: declaration is int X[?][32][8]. Option A has int X[32][32][8], which matches.

solution.result: A. X is declared as "int X[32][32][8]"

questions.formula_note: Row-major order address computation for 3D arrays: offset(X[i][j][k]) = i*(d2*d3*size) + j*(d3*size) + k*size; reverse-engineer dimensions from multipliers in generated IR.
